blocks.html_en:
> having lived through the dot-com hype in the 1990s

Having not lived through this, I'm actually curious as to how similar it was to the current hype cycle. Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?

I don't and wouldn't claim that AI is completely useless, I personally use it for at least a couple hours out of each working day and feel that it boosts my productivity significantly. My point is that the kind of benefits we're seeing from AI are not the ones we expected, and the difference between expectation and reality doesn't seem to have sunk in for most. I regularly hear friends talk of the post-scarcity future which is just around the corner, something like the culture Minds from Ian M. Banks, and this pseudoreligious narrative seems to be what the big AI players are deliberately selling. What I'm actually seeing is something more akin to Mr Meeseeks from Rick and Morty.

> So, you’re committed to falling for the next one, too, eh?

Haha, no. The point was that I've seen better things in the long winters between hype cycles than in any hype cycle.

sentences.en: > having lived through the dot-com hype in the 1990sHaving not lived through this, I'm actually curious as to how similar it was to the current hype cycle.
sentences.zh: > 经历过 20 世纪 90 年代的网络炒作虽然没有经历过这个，但我实际上很好奇它与当前的炒作周期有多么相似。

sentences.en: Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?I don't and wouldn't claim that AI is completely useless, I personally use it for at least a couple hours out of each working day and feel that it boosts my productivity significantly.
sentences.zh: 是否有一种普遍的感觉，类似奇点的东西正在逼近？我不认为也不会声称人工智能完全无用，我个人在每个工作日至少使用它几个小时，并觉得它显着提高了我的生产力。

sentences.en: My point is that the kind of benefits we're seeing from AI are not the ones we expected, and the difference between expectation and reality doesn't seem to have sunk in for most.
sentences.zh: 我的观点是，我们从人工智能中看到的好处并不是我们所期望的，而且大多数人似乎还没有意识到期望与现实之间的差异。

sentences.en: I regularly hear friends talk of the post-scarcity future which is just around the corner, something like the culture Minds from Ian M.
sentences.zh: 我经常听到朋友们谈论即将到来的后稀缺未来，就像 Ian M. 的文化思想一样。

sentences.en: Banks, and this pseudoreligious narrative seems to be what the big AI players are deliberately selling.
sentences.zh: 银行，而这种伪宗教叙事似乎是人工智能巨头故意推销的。

sentences.en: What I'm actually seeing is something more akin to Mr Meeseeks from Rick and Morty.> So, you’re committed to falling for the next one, too, eh?Haha, no.
sentences.zh: 我实际上看到的东西更类似于《瑞克和莫蒂》中的米塞克斯先生。> 所以，你也注定会爱上下一个，是吗？哈哈，不。

sentences.en: The point was that I've seen better things in the long winters between hype cycles than in any hype cycle.
sentences.zh: 关键是，我在炒作周期之间的漫长冬天看到了比任何炒作周期更好的事情。

blocks.html_en:
If you read the media from 1998 - 1999, there was than overarching message that EVERYTHING was changing. EVERY business needed to be on the Internet right now. Any business that wasn’t investing in the internet was going to be slaughtered. Brick and mortar stores were viewed as a negative on your balance sheet. All commerce would be e-commerce in just a couple of years. Startups with no revenue and no prospects for profitability for at least a decade were IPOing for hundreds of millions of dollars, sometimes with 2x-3x the valuation of brick and mortar peers in the same segment who had established brands going back decades. Whenever anyone questioned the lunacy, everyone responded that, “This time it’s different because the Internet changes everything. None of the old rules apply.”

And then the crash happened and we learned that all the old rules still did apply, and while the Internet was hugely transformative, the simplistic idea that brick and mortar was bad and e-commerce was the only way forward was fundamentally wrong. Yea, surely some segments did change (Amazon killing Borders and Waldenbooks, Netflix crushing Blockbuster, etc.), but investors ultimately look at profit even if they’re willing to ignore it for a while to grab market share.

So, I suspect that AI is going to play out similarly. Right now everyone is frothy, screaming that anyone not token maxing will die. Yes, AI is going to be impactful, and, yes, it’s not going away. But the hype is surely overblown. When was the singularity supposed to arrive? Three years ago, Altman was saying it was going to be 2025, right?

sentences.en: If you read the media from 1998 - 1999, there was than overarching message that EVERYTHING was changing.
sentences.zh: 如果您阅读 1998 年至 1999 年间的媒体，您会发现一切都在发生变化。

sentences.en: EVERY business needed to be on the Internet right now.
sentences.zh: 现在每个企业都需要上网。

sentences.en: Any business that wasn’t investing in the internet was going to be slaughtered.
sentences.zh: 任何不投资互联网的企业都将被屠杀。

sentences.en: Brick and mortar stores were viewed as a negative on your balance sheet.
sentences.zh: 实体店被视为资产负债表上的负面因素。

sentences.zh: 短短几年内，所有商业都将成为电子商务。

sentences.en: When was the singularity supposed to arrive?

blocks.html_en:
If you read the media from 1998 - 1999, there was than overarching message that EVERYTHING was changing. EVERY business needed to be on the Internet right now. Any business that wasn’t investing in the internet was going to be slaughtered. Brick and mortar stores were viewed as a negative on your balance sheet. All commerce would be e-commerce in just a couple of years. Startups with no revenue and no prospects for profitability for at least a decade were IPOing for hundreds of millions of dollars, sometimes with 2x-3x the valuation of brick and mortar peers in the same segment who had established brands going back decades. Whenever anyone questioned the lunacy, everyone responded that, “This time it’s different because the Internet changes everything. None of the old rules apply.”

And then the crash happened and we learned that all the old rules still did apply, and while the Internet was hugely transformative, the simplistic idea that brick and mortar was bad and e-commerce was the only way forward was fundamentally wrong. Yea, surely some segments did change (Amazon killing Borders and Waldenbooks, Netflix crushing Blockbuster, etc.), but investors ultimately look at profit even if they’re willing to ignore it for a while to grab market share.

So, I suspect that AI is going to play out similarly. Right now everyone is frothy, screaming that anyone not token maxing will die. Yes, AI is going to be impactful, and, yes, it’s not going away. But the hype is surely overblown. When was the singularity supposed to arrive? Three years ago, Altman was saying it was going to be 2025, right?

blocks.html_en:
> Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?

To a degree. There was breathless expectation that this was a turning point for commerce, government and society and that everyone had to stake their claim now. Everything was going to change.

What was it going to change into? Beyond "$OLDTHING but on the Internet!!!", nobody really knew. Those first steps were just webbifying existing processes, stumbling along, refining what was reasonably successful and dumping that which wasn't.

It was like a black hole; from afar we could vaguely see an event horizon, but the people falling into it had no idea what was happening or where they were in the process.

sentences.en: > Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?To a degree.
sentences.zh: > 是否存在一种普遍的感觉，即类似奇点的东西正在逼近？在某种程度上。

sentences.en: There was breathless expectation that this was a turning point for commerce, government and society and that everyone had to stake their claim now.

sentences.en: Everything was going to change.What was it going to change into?
sentences.zh: 一切都会改变。它会变成什么？

sentences.en: Beyond "$OLDTHING but on the Internet!!!", nobody really knew.
sentences.zh: 除了“$旧东西，但在互联网上！！！”之外，没有人真正知道。

sentences.en: Those first steps were just webbifying existing processes, stumbling along, refining what was reasonably successful and dumping that which wasn't.It was like a black hole; from afar we could vaguely see an event horizon, but the people falling into it had no idea what was happening or where they were in the process.
sentences.zh: 最初的步骤只是将现有流程网络化，跌跌撞撞地前进，完善那些相当成功的东西，然后抛弃那些不成功的东西。从远处我们可以隐约看到一个事件视界，但掉入其中的人们不知道发生了什么，也不知道他们在这个过程中处于什么位置。

blocks.html_en:
> Having not lived through this, I'm actually curious as to how similar it was to the current hype cycle. Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?

A singularity? No.

One of the early driving obsessions was a coalition dethroning Microsoft (which both did and didn't happen). A battle over closed-vs-open. That story was everywhere.

The main thing I notice is that there's a bit of a difference in the way value is created. Even in the UK the arrival of the web created a lot of early adopter winners — ISPs, design agencies, small dev shops like the one I worked for. And it generated a fair chunk of early money, changed the direction of state monopoly telecom etc.

Early well-bounded successes in 96-98 earned proper money, generated a bunch of press, generated new work. Because they had to: you didn't get access to huge pots of cash to grow with, until you had a product that was on the market and selling. The VCs didn't know you existed.

At the beginning it was small tech industry hustle and actual product creation, and a lot of companies really had the small software company playbook and ran that. You had a lot of PC resellers and systems builders who suddenly found themselves as qualified as anyone to build websites or run ISPs.

The problem after that was everyone's eyes got bigger than their bellies, the projects got too ambitious too soon, the VC money arrived even in the UK, and there were a lot of failures. I perceive the moment the firm I worked for got VC money to be the beginning of the end. I thought my bosses were altogether crazy to take the deal.

We basically were not ready to scale fast — the dot com era really scaled brand new companies in terms of number of employees and scope at a level that was at that time sort of unprecedented except maybe in the semiconductor industry.

Towards the pets.com IPO crash end of things, that is when it started to get unreal in a way that you'd recognise now — people talking about entirely new business models, and you had a running joke that if you just put "… on the internet" at the end of your elevator pitch people showered you with cash. The most insane of these being CueCat: a barcode scanner, but on the internet.

But did we think it would fuck everything up everywhere all at once? No. It was going to make us money and make it easier for everyone to buy and sell things, but it was going to create jobs (and it did for a while). It took until well into the early 2000s for the impact on retail to be truly felt.

Nowadays, companies scale enormously before they make any money, VC cash gets handed over to PhD students who only have the concepts of a plan, freemium tiers do the marketing and everyone is trying to follow the loss-leading market-share growth strategy that Amazon arguably pioneered.

The AI boom is perhaps the peak of that Amazon growth culture, "winner-takes-all" thinking, which was always insane.

sentences.zh: > 由于没有经历过这个，我实际上很好奇它与当前的炒作周期有多么相似。

sentences.en: It took until well into the early 2000s for the impact on retail to be truly felt.Nowadays, companies scale enormously before they make any money, VC cash gets handed over to PhD students who only have the concepts of a plan, freemium tiers do the marketing and everyone is trying to follow the loss-leading market-share growth strategy that Amazon arguably pioneered.The AI boom is perhaps the peak of that Amazon growth culture, "winner-takes-all" thinking, which was always insane.
sentences.zh: 直到 2000 年代初，人们才真正感受到对零售业的影响。如今，公司在赚钱之前就已经规模巨大，风险投资现金被交给了只有计划概念的博士生，免费增值层进行营销，每个人都在努力遵循亚马逊可以说是开创性的亏损领先的市场份额增长战略。人工智能的繁荣也许是亚马逊增长文化的顶峰，“赢者通吃”的思维一直是亚马逊的发展文化的顶峰。疯了。

blocks.html_en:
> Having not lived through this, I'm actually curious as to how similar it was to the current hype cycle. Was there a general sense that something singularity-like was approaching?

A singularity? No.

One of the early driving obsessions was a coalition dethroning Microsoft (which both did and didn't happen). A battle over closed-vs-open. That story was everywhere.

The main thing I notice is that there's a bit of a difference in the way value is created. Even in the UK the arrival of the web created a lot of early adopter winners — ISPs, design agencies, small dev shops like the one I worked for. And it generated a fair chunk of early money, changed the direction of state monopoly telecom etc.

Early well-bounded successes in 96-98 earned proper money, generated a bunch of press, generated new work. Because they had to: you didn't get access to huge pots of cash to grow with, until you had a product that was on the market and selling. The VCs didn't know you existed.

At the beginning it was small tech industry hustle and actual product creation, and a lot of companies really had the small software company playbook and ran that. You had a lot of PC resellers and systems builders who suddenly found themselves as qualified as anyone to build websites or run ISPs.

The problem after that was everyone's eyes got bigger than their bellies, the projects got too ambitious too soon, the VC money arrived even in the UK, and there were a lot of failures. I perceive the moment the firm I worked for got VC money to be the beginning of the end. I thought my bosses were altogether crazy to take the deal.

We basically were not ready to scale fast — the dot com era really scaled brand new companies in terms of number of employees and scope at a level that was at that time sort of unprecedented except maybe in the semiconductor industry.

Towards the pets.com IPO crash end of things, that is when it started to get unreal in a way that you'd recognise now — people talking about entirely new business models, and you had a running joke that if you just put "… on the internet" at the end of your elevator pitch people showered you with cash. The most insane of these being CueCat: a barcode scanner, but on the internet.

But did we think it would fuck everything up everywhere all at once? No. It was going to make us money and make it easier for everyone to buy and sell things, but it was going to create jobs (and it did for a while). It took until well into the early 2000s for the impact on retail to be truly felt.

Nowadays, companies scale enormously before they make any money, VC cash gets handed over to PhD students who only have the concepts of a plan, freemium tiers do the marketing and everyone is trying to follow the loss-leading market-share growth strategy that Amazon arguably pioneered.

The AI boom is perhaps the peak of that Amazon growth culture, "winner-takes-all" thinking, which was always insane.

sentences.en: > Having not lived through this, I'm actually curious as to how similar it was to the current hype cycle.
sentences.zh: > 由于没有经历过这个，我实际上很好奇它与当前的炒作周期有多么相似。

blocks.html_en: Each new iteration of the hype cycle feels "like its not hype" because the tech is more promising in new ways, but the hype comes from human perception. New tech has to fight a lot of real world and project management issues, and development morphs and slows. We don't know exactly what we have until it faces these issues.

sentences.zh: 炒作周期的每一次新迭代都让人感觉“不像是炒作”，因为这项技术以新的方式更有前途，但炒作来自人类的感知。

sentences.en: New tech has to fight a lot of real world and project management issues, and development morphs and slows.
sentences.zh: 新技术必须应对许多现实世界和项目管理问题，并且开发会发生变化和减慢。

sentences.en: We don't know exactly what we have until it faces these issues.
sentences.zh: 在面临这些问题之前，我们并不知道我们到底拥有什么。

blocks.html_en:
I totally agree with all your points except welcoming the singularity.

How do you imagine your or your children's lives in the singularity?

What do you do there for a living, or which hobbies do you pursue in the best case it frees you and everyone around to pursuit nothing but hobbies?

What do you do in the worst case it pushes everyone to underground strongholds to fight machines for the survival of the humankind?

And if there's some middle ground, how is it different from today's state of affairs?

blocks.html_en:
> It hasn't worked out like that

It seems quite premature to say that. We're 3 to 4 years into the LLM revolution and the rate of progress is still impressive. The recursive self-improvement aspect that is necessary for the actual singularity is something we're only really starting to get into this year.

If the singularity is 5 years from now, that is still much sooner than most people (including me) previously expected it to happen.

sentences.en: > It hasn't worked out like thatIt seems quite premature to say that.
sentences.zh: > 事情并没有那么顺利 这么说似乎还为时过早。

sentences.en: We're 3 to 4 years into the LLM revolution and the rate of progress is still impressive.
sentences.zh: 我们的法学硕士革命已经进行了 3 到 4 年，进展速度仍然令人印象深刻。

sentences.en: The recursive self-improvement aspect that is necessary for the actual singularity is something we're only really starting to get into this year.If the singularity is 5 years from now, that is still much sooner than most people (including me) previously expected it to happen.

blocks.html_en:
> We're 3 to 4 years into the LLM revolution and the rate of progress is still impressive.

The “Attention Is All You Need” paper came out in 2017 and OpenAI released GPT-1 in 2018. ChatGPT was released in late 2022 but that was not the beginning of LLMs. Transformers and generative AI go back even further.

blocks.html_en:
> It seems quite premature to say that. We're 3 to 4 years into the LLM revolution and the rate of progress is still impressive.

When we were 3 to 4 years into mobile phone revolution it was already abundantly clear that it's changing the way people leave.

Ditto when mobile phones became smartphones, and for many other tech items we got in the past decades.

As for LLMs... outside of a few precious professions you could live your life and not notice they are there.

sentences.en: > It seems quite premature to say that.
sentences.zh: > 现在这么说似乎还为时过早。

sentences.zh: 除了一些宝贵的职业之外，你可能会过着自己的生活而没有注意到它们的存在。

blocks.html_en:
The first prototype of a cellphone was functional in the early 70s. Before that you had walkie talkies and farther back wireless communications.

We're just shy of 10 years since attention is all you need and just a few years past the first commercially viable LLMs. So in cellphones we are something like early 90s.

blocks.html_en: We're just shy of 10 years since attention is all you need and just a few years past the first commercially viable LLMs. So in cellphones we are something like early 90s.

sentences.en: We're just shy of 10 years since attention is all you need and just a few years past the first commercially viable LLMs.
sentences.zh: 我们距离第一个商业上可行的法学硕士的诞生还有不到 10 年的时间，因为注意力就是您所需要的一切。

sentences.en: So in cellphones we are something like early 90s.
sentences.zh: 所以在手机领域，我们就像是 90 年代初。

blocks.html_en:
What they're saying is that mobile phone tech didn't scale at the same rate as LLMs. How fast it takes a technology to unfold into full adoption varies depending on the industry and on what's blocking development at its inception.

Mobile phones were severely constrained by hardware that made them impractically expensive and unwieldy, and also by the time it took to set up wireless networks at a large enough scale. That's why it took mobile phones a few decades to really get going, and this is going to be different for every other technology.

LLMs, for instance, were not as hardware-constrained - we have a lot more compute now, but there wasn't a hardware paradigm shift or anything, things were already pretty good a few years ago - now it's just about scale. LLMs were also always relatively affordable - there wasn't a period where they cost $10/token, so we don't get the same kind of affordability scaling as with phones. They also utilize existing internet infrastructure and don't need to be rolled out in some new worldwide grid.

sentences.en: What they're saying is that mobile phone tech didn't scale at the same rate as LLMs.
sentences.zh: 他们的意思是，手机技术的发展速度不如法学硕士。

sentences.en: How fast it takes a technology to unfold into full adoption varies depending on the industry and on what's blocking development at its inception.Mobile phones were severely constrained by hardware that made them impractically expensive and unwieldy, and also by the time it took to set up wireless networks at a large enough scale.
sentences.zh: 一项技术需要多快才能得到全面采用，具体取决于行业以及其发展初期的阻碍因素。移动电话受到硬件的严重限制，使其变得昂贵且笨重，不切实际，而且还需要建立足够大规模的无线网络。

sentences.en: That's why it took mobile phones a few decades to really get going, and this is going to be different for every other technology.LLMs, for instance, were not as hardware-constrained - we have a lot more compute now, but there wasn't a hardware paradigm shift or anything, things were already pretty good a few years ago - now it's just about scale.
sentences.zh: 这就是为什么手机花了几十年才真正开始发展，而这对于其他技术来说是不同的。例如，法学硕士不受硬件限制——我们现在有更多的计算能力，但没有硬件范式转变或任何东西，几年前情况已经相当不错了——现在只是规模问题。

sentences.en: LLMs were also always relatively affordable - there wasn't a period where they cost $10/token, so we don't get the same kind of affordability scaling as with phones.
sentences.zh: LLM 也总是相对便宜 - 没有一段时期它们的价格为 10 美元/代币，因此我们无法获得与手机相同的负担能力扩展。

sentences.zh: 它们还利用现有的互联网基础设施，不需要在一些新的全球网格中推广。

blocks.html_en:
I think this is off. LLMs are severely constrained by hardware and we've seen at least an order of magnitude improvement in flops since ChatGPT launched. Over the next couple of decades we are likely to see that level of improvement in memory capacity. There's likely at least a few step changes left.

Regardless, the point of the GP that we saw revolutionary changes in society within a few years of technology introduction is off. It took decades for the full impact of the pc, internet, or mobile to be seen. We're still in a very early phase of figuring out how to use LLMs.

sentences.en: I think this is off.
sentences.zh: 我觉得这个没戏了

sentences.en: Over the next couple of decades we are likely to see that level of improvement in memory capacity.
sentences.zh: 在接下来的几十年里，我们可能会看到记忆容量的提高。

sentences.zh: 可能至少还剩下几步变化。无论如何，GP 的观点是，我们在技术引入的几年内看到了社会的革命性变化，这是不正确的。

sentences.en: It took decades for the full impact of the pc, internet, or mobile to be seen.
sentences.zh: 电脑、互联网或移动设备的全面影响花了几十年的时间才显现出来。

sentences.en: We're still in a very early phase of figuring out how to use LLMs.
sentences.zh: 我们仍处于弄清楚如何使用法学硕士的早期阶段。

blocks.html_en:
> As for LLMs... outside of a few precious professions you could live your life and not notice they are there.

And old folks said the same thing about the internet and smart phones. Fads that would pass. While the younger generations lived an entirely different lifestyle.

I’m personally seeing the same. I resisted AI being a “thing” for a long while, but since I’m around a lot of teens and young adults it was a choice of burying my head in the sand or deciding to take a look.

They are going to change society on a scale far beyond smart phones. In ways many will not see as positives, but change never goes the way one may expect.

sentences.en: > As for LLMs...
sentences.zh: > 至于法学硕士...

sentences.en: outside of a few precious professions you could live your life and not notice they are there.And old folks said the same thing about the internet and smart phones.
sentences.zh: 除了一些宝贵的职业之外，你可以过自己的生活而不会注意到它们的存在。老人们对互联网和智能手机也说了同样的话。

sentences.en: Fads that would pass.
sentences.zh: 那些会过去的时尚。

sentences.en: While the younger generations lived an entirely different lifestyle.I’m personally seeing the same.
sentences.zh: 虽然年轻一代过着完全不同的生活方式，但我个人也看到了同样的情况。

sentences.en: I resisted AI being a “thing” for a long while, but since I’m around a lot of teens and young adults it was a choice of burying my head in the sand or deciding to take a look.They are going to change society on a scale far beyond smart phones.
sentences.zh: 很长一段时间以来，我一直拒绝将人工智能视为一种“事物”，但由于我周围有很多青少年和年轻人，所以我可以选择把头埋在沙子里，或者决定看看。它们将改变社会，其规模将远远超出智能手机。

sentences.en: In ways many will not see as positives, but change never goes the way one may expect.
sentences.zh: 在很多方面，许多人不会认为这是积极的，但变化永远不会像人们预期的那样发展。

> "And old folks said the same thing about the internet and smart phones."

Ironic how LLM promoters like to hallucinate citations, much like the tools they espouse.

blocks.html_en:
It's clear that while LLM progress is continuing, it's at something like a linear rate, or slower.

The singularity requires exponential improvement. That's not happening. Maybe we will develop something that sets that off, but there's no indication of that right now.

sentences.en: It's clear that while LLM progress is continuing, it's at something like a linear rate, or slower.The singularity requires exponential improvement.
sentences.zh: 很明显，虽然法学硕士的进步仍在继续，但它的速度类似于线性速度，或者更慢。奇点需要指数级的改进。

sentences.en: That's not happening.
sentences.zh: 那并没有发生。

blocks.html_en:
It's clear that while LLM progress is continuing, it's at something like a linear rate, or slower.

The singularity requires exponential improvement. That's not happening. Maybe we will develop something that sets that off, but there's no indication of that right now.

blocks.html_en:
Yet. If the exponential improvement had already started, the singularity would already have happened or happen very, very soon.

The logic has always been that the AI would have to have significant tools and agency to do self-improvement for the singularity to occur. This is exactly the thing that a bunch of the AI labs are working on hard right now.

blocks.html_en:
As a vocal advocate for the use of machine intelligence in certain contexts and a skeptic of claims they are useless or always hallucinate or whatever...

It hasn't been even close to even the recent claims! It's been like three straight years where it was supposed to utterly transform society any minute now.

I think we more or less have a fragile consensus that in like, computer programming and really very little else, that on a good day you're probably going to come out ahead with the AI assist. That seems relatively uncontroversial now. But it also seems like success with AI is largely about working hard to get good at it, as a first order concern. It is not at all obvious that blind, uncritical use by anyone is a net win.

It's pretty unclear, sone might say dubious, that anyone has made serious, aboveboard money net of debt and equity, other than the hardware vendors. There's some pretty serious revenue, but it's a drop in the proverbial bucket against the outlay. There like a two trillion dollar balance sheet hole in the US alone where investment into AI has gone.

While I personally don't agree with them, multiple S-tier machine learning researchers think we've got our wheels stuck in the mud, that autoregressive decoder architectures on a tokin-suffix pre-train is tapped out as a paradigm.

It's ok to say "AI is starting to get useful in pretty durable ways" and not sound like an Anthropic shareholder/employee, i.e. completely full of shit.

sentences.en: As a vocal advocate for the use of machine intelligence in certain contexts and a skeptic of claims they are useless or always hallucinate or whatever...It hasn't been even close to even the recent claims!
sentences.zh: 作为在某些情况下使用机器智能的直言不讳的倡导者，以及对机器智能无用或总是产生幻觉等说法的怀疑论者……它甚至与最近的说法相去甚远！

blocks.html_en:
I don't think anyone's disputing that the models are better than a year ago (though clearly all this, recursive self improvement stuff is utterly hypothetical and that's being generous, most of the progress has been on cost and fit and finish stuff). When it's on the plan, I'll use Fable for some stuff. If I'm paying for Opus? It's 4.5 or 4.6 which were dramatically better aligned and token efficient in the trace at a capability gap that's "you win some and lose some".

That can be true while it also being the case that to someone who has no idea how this stuff works under the hood, it's basically Dunning Kreuger in a box. The next person to go /u/PhdInEverything on me with Fable is getting an education in the history of hardware support for mixed precision training or something. Fable is a masterclass in refusal to ground and a dozen other alignment catastrophes.

So yeah, the models are still getting a little better, but from here out I think it's rapidly becoming a skill game.

sentences.en: I don't think anyone's disputing that the models are better than a year ago (though clearly all this, recursive self improvement stuff is utterly hypothetical and that's being generous, most of the progress has been on cost and fit and finish stuff).
sentences.zh: 我认为没有人会质疑这些模型比一年前更好（尽管显然所有这些，递归自我改进的东西完全是假设的，而且这是慷慨的，大部分进展都是在成本、安装和完成方面）。

sentences.en: When it's on the plan, I'll use Fable for some stuff.
sentences.zh: 当它在计划中时，我会用《寓言》来做一些事情。

sentences.en: If I'm paying for Opus?
sentences.zh: 如果我支付 Opus 费用？

sentences.en: It's 4.5 or 4.6 which were dramatically better aligned and token efficient in the trace at a capability gap that's "you win some and lose some".That can be true while it also being the case that to someone who has no idea how this stuff works under the hood, it's basically Dunning Kreuger in a box.
sentences.zh: 4.5 或 4.6 在能力差距的跟踪中明显更好地对齐和令牌效率，即“你赢了一些，输了一些”。这可能是真的，但对于那些不知道这些东西在幕后如何工作的人来说，它基本上是盒子里的 Dunning Kreuger。

sentences.en: The next person to go /u/PhdInEverything on me with Fable is getting an education in the history of hardware support for mixed precision training or something.

sentences.en: Fable is a masterclass in refusal to ground and a dozen other alignment catastrophes.So yeah, the models are still getting a little better, but from here out I think it's rapidly becoming a skill game.
sentences.zh: 《神鬼寓言》是拒绝地面和其他十几种对齐灾难的大师班。所以，是的，模型仍然变得更好，但从这里开始，我认为它正在迅速成为一个技能游戏。

sentences.zh: 《神鬼寓言》是拒绝地面和其他十几种对齐灾难的大师班。所以，是的，模型仍然变得更好，但从这里开始，我认为它正在迅速成为一个技能游戏。

blocks.html_en: I don't think anyone's disputing that the models are better than a year ago (though clearly all this, recursive self improvement stuff is utterly hypothetical and that's being generous, most of the progress has been on cost and fit and finish stuff). When it's on the plan, I'll use Fable for some stuff. If I'm paying for Opus? It's 4.5 or 4.6 which were dramatically better aligned and token efficient in the trace at a capability gap that's "you win some and lose some".

sentences.en: I don't think anyone's disputing that the models are better than a year ago (though clearly all this, recursive self improvement stuff is utterly hypothetical and that's being generous, most of the progress has been on cost and fit and finish stuff).
sentences.zh: 我认为没有人会质疑这些模型比一年前更好（尽管显然所有这些，递归自我改进的东西完全是假设的，而且这是慷慨的，大部分进展都是在成本、安装和完成方面）。

sentences.en: When it's on the plan, I'll use Fable for some stuff.
sentences.zh: 当它在计划中时，我会用《寓言》来做一些事情。

sentences.en: If I'm paying for Opus?
sentences.zh: 如果我支付 Opus 费用？

sentences.en: It's 4.5 or 4.6 which were dramatically better aligned and token efficient in the trace at a capability gap that's "you win some and lose some".
sentences.zh: 4.5 或 4.6 在跟踪中的一致性和令牌效率显着提高，但能力差距是“你赢得了一些，失去了一些”。

blocks.html_en: That can be true while it also being the case that to someone who has no idea how this stuff works under the hood, it's basically Dunning Kreuger in a box. The next person to go /u/PhdInEverything on me with Fable is getting an education in the history of hardware support for mixed precision training or something. Fable is a masterclass in refusal to ground and a dozen other alignment catastrophes.

sentences.en: That can be true while it also being the case that to someone who has no idea how this stuff works under the hood, it's basically Dunning Kreuger in a box.
sentences.zh: 这可能是真的，但对于那些不知道这些东西在幕后如何工作的人来说，它基本上就是盒子里的 Dunning Kreuger。

sentences.en: The next person to go /u/PhdInEverything on me with Fable is getting an education in the history of hardware support for mixed precision training or something.
sentences.zh: 下一个与《神鬼寓言》一起去 /u/PhdInEverything 的人正在接受混合精度训练或其他硬件支持历史的教育。

sentences.en: Fable is a masterclass in refusal to ground and a dozen other alignment catastrophes.
sentences.zh: 《神鬼寓言》是关于拒绝接地和其他十几种对齐灾难的大师级作品。

blocks.html_en: So yeah, the models are still getting a little better, but from here out I think it's rapidly becoming a skill game.

sentences.en: So yeah, the models are still getting a little better, but from here out I think it's rapidly becoming a skill game.
sentences.zh: 所以，是的，模型仍然在变得更好，但从现在开始，我认为它正在迅速成为一个技能游戏。

blocks.html_en:
It was the same thing with blockchain, who here remembers the peak delusion of IoT + smart contracts replacing human judicial systems and crypto making central banks useless?

LLMs are of course a form of progress, the same way blockchain was, but it has been baffling and cringy nonetheless to see people with a strong technical background pretending that at some point in the future the development of complex applications was going to be reduced to a few prompt lines written in English.

Once the AI fad dies and the next hype appears I told myself I'm going to jump on it to take financial advantage of it. At this point I believe that the technological cycle of hype is an almost entirely social/economic phenomenon and there is no reason not to make money from it given that people insist on this kind of irrational behavior.

sentences.en: It was the same thing with blockchain, who here remembers the peak delusion of IoT + smart contracts replacing human judicial systems and crypto making central banks useless?LLMs are of course a form of progress, the same way blockchain was, but it has been baffling and cringy nonetheless to see people with a strong technical background pretending that at some point in the future the development of complex applications was going to be reduced to a few prompt lines written in English.Once the AI fad dies and the next hype appears I told myself I'm going to jump on it to take financial advantage of it.
sentences.zh: 区块链也是如此，谁还记得物联网+智能合约取代人类司法系统和加密货币让中央银行变得毫无用处的巅峰错觉？法学硕士当然是一种进步形式，就像区块链一样，但看到具有强大技术背景的人假装在未来的某个时候复杂应用程序的开发将被简化为用英语编写的几行提示线，这仍然令人困惑和尴尬。一旦人工智能时尚消亡，下一个炒作就会出现，我告诉就我自己而言，我会抓住它来利用它的经济优势。

sentences.en: At this point I believe that the technological cycle of hype is an almost entirely social/economic phenomenon and there is no reason not to make money from it given that people insist on this kind of irrational behavior.
sentences.zh: 在这一点上，我认为炒作的技术周期几乎完全是一种社会/经济现象，鉴于人们坚持这种非理性行为，没有理由不从中赚钱。

blocks.html_en:
> Most of us on some level felt confident that AI would completely revolutionise our society.

I don't want to sound like a jerk, so please let me know if I do - I don't understand why you claim that "most of us" felt that way. I ignored "AI" until mid 2024. Then I tried it and was very unimpressed. I tried again in 2026. Seemed useful, then very quickly I realized that it's something that looks great until you look closer.

I was never impressed by a text generator. A talking computer might be the most boring thing I could imagine. I never understood why people got so obsessed by it and I certainly don't understand why it would "change society". Society is composed of people. What would change society is something that would allow people to think less, feel more clearly, express themselves more freely, and suffer less. Of course, things that accomplish these results exist, but they require a lot of effort. I cannot fathom how a technology that doesn't lead to these results could revolutionize anything. In fact, every technology, that I witnessed arising since late 90s has made things worse precisely because it has not done anything to address the human condition, instead praying on it.

sentences.en: > Most of us on some level felt confident that AI would completely revolutionise our society.I don't want to sound like a jerk, so please let me know if I do - I don't understand why you claim that "most of us" felt that way.
sentences.zh: > 我们大多数人在某种程度上都相信人工智能将彻底改变我们的社会。我不想听起来像个混蛋，所以如果我这样做，请告诉我 - 我不明白为什么你声称“我们大多数人”有这种感觉。

sentences.en: I ignored "AI" until mid 2024.
sentences.zh: 直到 2024 年中期我才忽略“人工智能”。

sentences.en: Then I tried it and was very unimpressed.
sentences.zh: 然后我尝试了一下，结果非常不以为然。

sentences.en: I tried again in 2026.
sentences.zh: 我在 2026 年再次尝试。

sentences.en: Seemed useful, then very quickly I realized that it's something that looks great until you look closer.I was never impressed by a text generator.
sentences.zh: 看起来很有用，然后很快我就意识到它看起来很棒，直到你仔细观察。文本生成器从来没有给我留下深刻的印象。

sentences.en: A talking computer might be the most boring thing I could imagine.
sentences.zh: 会说话的计算机可能是我能想象到的最无聊的东西。

sentences.en: I never understood why people got so obsessed by it and I certainly don't understand why it would "change society".
sentences.zh: 我从来不明白为什么人们对它如此着迷，我当然也不明白为什么它会“改变社会”。

sentences.en: Society is composed of people.
sentences.zh: 社会是由人组成的。

sentences.en: What would change society is something that would allow people to think less, feel more clearly, express themselves more freely, and suffer less.
sentences.zh: 改变社会的是让人们思考更少、感受更清晰、更自由地表达自己、减少痛苦。

sentences.en: Of course, things that accomplish these results exist, but they require a lot of effort.
sentences.zh: 当然，实现这些结果的事情是存在的，但需要付出很多努力。

sentences.en: I cannot fathom how a technology that doesn't lead to these results could revolutionize anything.
sentences.zh: 我无法理解一项不能带来这些结果的技术如何能够彻底改变任何事物。

sentences.en: In fact, every technology, that I witnessed arising since late 90s has made things worse precisely because it has not done anything to address the human condition, instead praying on it.
sentences.zh: 事实上，自 90 年代末以来我亲眼目睹的每一项技术的出现都让事情变得更糟，因为它没有做任何事情来解决人类的状况，而是祈祷人类的状况。

blocks.html_en:
We are still in time for the "revolution" ... but it will take longer and won't look like than most thought.

We still need a bubble pop to drive out all nonsense ideas and paths.

sentences.zh: 但这需要更长的时间，而且不会像大多数人想象的那样。我们仍然需要一个泡沫来消除所有无意义的想法和路径。

blocks.html_en:
This part toward the end of the article resonated with me:

> If you’re being asked to review huge volumes of terrible AI code, just assume that the organisation is going to burn you out and fire you. You will not convince the person drowning you in 2000 line PRs to stop. Start looking for a new job as if you have already been fired. I have seen this happen many times now

I suspect we will see this phenomenon more and more as organizations more widely adopt agentic development.

sentences.zh: 开始寻找新工作，就像您已经被解雇一样。

blocks.html_en: I don’t buy it - if you’re an engineering leader and this is happening it’s your responsibility to get out there and fix the problem through education, process improvements, improved automated quality checks, better planning. Sticking your fingers in your ears and shouting “no!” is not a sustainable strategy. Figure out what would allow your throughput to double, triple etc and go organise it. Focus on bottlenecks and the most difficult pieces. I’m speaking from active experience here.

blocks.html_en:
« it's telling that you perceive any criticism of AI code reviewer burnout as "sticking your fingers in your ears" »

Go back and re-read the quote from your comment - which I responded to: “ You will not convince the person drowning you in 2000 line PRs to stop. Start looking for a new job as if you have already been fired.”

I’m not overreacting to some nuanced take here. I’m not sure how to characterise quitting as a response to over-enthusiasm / slop as anything other than the career equivalent of sticking your fingers in your ears. I’ve been there, I’ve had the arguments, I have little sympathy for staff/principal engineers or above who react like this - their job is to fix the organisation, put a yoke around the beast of AI and find a way to tap the benefits and use the problems (like those 2000 line PRs) as a golden signal for where you need to educate and improve process and planning.

Again - I’m not selling anything here, I just have found it an interesting challenge over the last year and one where I’ve been happy to make progress organisationally despite the frustrating moments. If someone’s response to a problem like this is to throw their hands up in despair or to engage in whatever the logical opposite of AI-hype is (which the article resorts to repeatedly) then yes I’d worry about being laid off. If you’re in an organisation where you don’t have the agency to fix problems then absolutely - start looking for a new job.

sentences.en: « it's telling that you perceive any criticism of AI code reviewer burnout as "sticking your fingers in your ears" »Go back and re-read the quote from your comment - which I responded to: “ You will not convince the person drowning you in 2000 line PRs to stop.
sentences.zh: « 这表明你认为任何对 AI 代码审查员倦怠的批评都是“把手指放在耳朵里” »返回并重新阅读你的评论中的引文 - 我对此做出了回应：“你无法说服那个在 2000 行 PR 中淹没你的人停止。

sentences.en: Start looking for a new job as if you have already been fired.”I’m not overreacting to some nuanced take here.
sentences.zh: 开始寻找新工作，就好像你已经被解雇了一样。”我并没有对这里的一些细微差别反应过度。

sentences.en: I’m not sure how to characterise quitting as a response to over-enthusiasm / slop as anything other than the career equivalent of sticking your fingers in your ears.
sentences.zh: 我不知道如何将辞职描述为对过度热情/失职的反应，而不是职业生涯中将手指放在耳朵里的反应。

sentences.en: I’ve been there, I’ve had the arguments, I have little sympathy for staff/principal engineers or above who react like this - their job is to fix the organisation, put a yoke around the beast of AI and find a way to tap the benefits and use the problems (like those 2000 line PRs) as a golden signal for where you need to educate and improve process and planning.Again - I’m not selling anything here, I just have found it an interesting challenge over the last year and one where I’ve been happy to make progress organisationally despite the frustrating moments.
sentences.zh: 我去过那里，我也有过争论，我对员工/首席工程师或以上的人的反应没有什么同情心——他们的工作是修复组织，给人工智能的野兽套上枷锁，找到一种方法来利用好处并使用问题（比如那些 2000 行 PR）作为你需要教育和改进流程和规划的黄金信号。再说一次——我不是在这里卖任何东西，我只是在过去的一年里发现这是一个有趣的挑战尽管经历了令人沮丧的时刻，但我很高兴在组织上取得了进展。

sentences.en: If someone’s response to a problem like this is to throw their hands up in despair or to engage in whatever the logical opposite of AI-hype is (which the article resorts to repeatedly) then yes I’d worry about being laid off.
sentences.zh: 如果有人对这样的问题的反应是绝望地举手，或者从事任何与人工智能炒作逻辑相反的事情（本文反复诉诸），那么是的，我会担心被解雇。

sentences.en: If you’re in an organisation where you don’t have the agency to fix problems then absolutely - start looking for a new job.
sentences.zh: 如果你所在的组织没有解决问题的机构，那么绝对要开始寻找新工作。

blocks.html_en: One of the key things I've needed to educate people on is that code contributions are no longer valuable in and of themselves. Someone who produces a large volume of bad code does not have high throughput that needs to be allowed, they have low throughput that needs to be corrected. High throughput in software is now almost entirely about having the right ideas and analysis.

sentences.en: One of the key things I've needed to educate people on is that code contributions are no longer valuable in and of themselves.
sentences.zh: 我需要教育人们的关键事情之一是代码贡献本身不再有价值。

sentences.en: Someone who produces a large volume of bad code does not have high throughput that needs to be allowed, they have low throughput that needs to be corrected.
sentences.zh: 产生大量不良代码的人并不具有需要允许的高吞吐量，他们具有需要纠正的低吞吐量。

sentences.en: High throughput in software is now almost entirely about having the right ideas and analysis.
sentences.zh: 现在，软件的高吞吐量几乎完全取决于正确的想法和分析。

blocks.html_en: One of the key things I've needed to educate people on is that code contributions are no longer valuable in and of themselves. Someone who produces a large volume of bad code does not have high throughput that needs to be allowed, they have low throughput that needs to be corrected. High throughput in software is now almost entirely about having the right ideas and analysis.

blocks.html_en:
> All of the AI projects we have observed as a team are failing. Every single one – we have seen 0% success in a year and a half, not only amongst projects we have been asked to participate in, but even within projects that we have observed in passing while doing totally unrelated work.

That's got to be hyperbole, which blows out their credibility. They chose to say 'AI' rather than, for example, LLM, or Transformer model, or Diffusion model. This means they are including a huge swathe of things dating back to Expert Systems in their claim.

And who hasn't seen productivity gains from more established AI technology - at least things like semantic search? Who hasn't seen diffusion models generating content in roles that might have done the work by hand before? Who hasn't seen some kind of regression algorithm (even using linear regression in a supervised context counts as AI - so you can absolutely do AI even in tools like Excel) improve operation productivity?

Even if they narrowed it to the Transformer model LLMs which re-ignited recent public interest in AI, less ambitious projects to give them to engineering staff to automate easy but boring tasks in the background generally have been a success. More ambitious ones that are beyond what you'd reasonably expect the models to be able to do - for sure, those tend to fail. For most of these, the failure is predictable in advance, while some are at the boundary of what's possible, and so it is harder to predict (these are rationally genuine R&D projects).

sentences.en: > All of the AI projects we have observed as a team are failing.
sentences.zh: > 我们作为一个团队观察到的所有人工智能项目都失败了。

sentences.en: Every single one – we have seen 0% success in a year and a half, not only amongst projects we have been asked to participate in, but even within projects that we have observed in passing while doing totally unrelated work.That's got to be hyperbole, which blows out their credibility.
sentences.zh: 每一个项目——我们在一年半的时间里都看到了 0% 的成功，不仅是在我们被要求参与的项目中，甚至在我们在做完全不相关的工作时顺便观察到的项目中也是如此。这一定是夸张的，这破坏了他们的可信度。

sentences.en: They chose to say 'AI' rather than, for example, LLM, or Transformer model, or Diffusion model.
sentences.zh: 他们选择说“AI”，而不是 LLM、Transformer 模型或 Diffusion 模型等。

sentences.en: This means they are including a huge swathe of things dating back to Expert Systems in their claim.And who hasn't seen productivity gains from more established AI technology - at least things like semantic search?
sentences.zh: 这意味着他们在声明中包含了大量可以追溯到专家系统的东西。谁没有看到更成熟的人工智能技术提高了生产力——至少是语义搜索之类的东西？

sentences.en: Who hasn't seen diffusion models generating content in roles that might have done the work by hand before?
sentences.zh: 谁没有见过扩散模型在以前可能需要手工完成工作的角色中生成内容？

sentences.en: Who hasn't seen some kind of regression algorithm (even using linear regression in a supervised context counts as AI - so you can absolutely do AI even in tools like Excel) improve operation productivity?Even if they narrowed it to the Transformer model LLMs which re-ignited recent public interest in AI, less ambitious projects to give them to engineering staff to automate easy but boring tasks in the background generally have been a success.
sentences.zh: 谁没有见过某种回归算法（即使在监督环境中使用线性回归也算作人工智能——所以即使在 Excel 等工具中也绝对可以实现人工智能）提高操作生产力？即使他们将其范围缩小到 Transformer 模型法学硕士，重新点燃了公众最近对人工智能的兴趣，但不太雄心勃勃的项目让工程人员在后台自动执行简单但无聊的任务通常是成功的。

sentences.en: More ambitious ones that are beyond what you'd reasonably expect the models to be able to do - for sure, those tend to fail.
sentences.zh: 更雄心勃勃的目标超出了你对模型能力的合理预期——当然，这些目标往往会失败。

sentences.en: For most of these, the failure is predictable in advance, while some are at the boundary of what's possible, and so it is harder to predict (these are rationally genuine R&D projects).
sentences.zh: 对于其中大多数项目，失败是可以提前预测的，而有些则处于可能的边界，因此更难预测（这些是理性上真正的研发项目）。

blocks.html_en:
If you read the footnote, they follow up to say they've rejected 100% of the AI projects brought to them.

Go to their home page and one of their consulting selling points is recovering struggling projects.

One of their front-page selling points is that they use "ancient techniques" from books written prior to the year 2000, because presumably everything newer than that is bad?

> For non-executive management who might be struggling to deliver things that feel beyond their control, we have ancient techniques (see: books written between 1986 and 1999) to turn your team into the envy of the organisation, and we can drop in directly to get your team the resources it needs to save a struggling project.

This is entirely a selection bias issue that they've created for themselves: Advertise a consulting service for saving failing projects to companies that don't have internal expertise to handle it, then write blog posts that 100% of the projects you see are failing. Also refuse to help them, to guarantee they can't be converted to successful projects to keep the success number at 0%.

sentences.en: If you read the footnote, they follow up to say they've rejected 100% of the AI projects brought to them.Go to their home page and one of their consulting selling points is recovering struggling projects.One of their front-page selling points is that they use "ancient techniques" from books written prior to the year 2000, because presumably everything newer than that is bad?> For non-executive management who might be struggling to deliver things that feel beyond their control, we have ancient techniques (see: books written between 1986 and 1999) to turn your team into the envy of the organisation, and we can drop in directly to get your team the resources it needs to save a struggling project.This is entirely a selection bias issue that they've created for themselves: Advertise a consulting service for saving failing projects to companies that don't have internal expertise to handle it, then write blog posts that 100% of the projects you see are failing.
sentences.zh: 如果你读脚注，他们会接着说他们已经拒绝了 100% 的人工智能项目。转到他们的主页，他们的咨询卖点之一是恢复陷入困境的项目。他们的头版卖点之一是他们使用 2000 年之前写的书中的“古老技术”，因为大概所有比这更新的东西都是不好的？> 对于那些可能正在努力交付感觉超出他们控制范围的事情的非执行管理人员，我们有古老的技术（请参阅： 1986 年至 1999 年间写的书）让您的团队成为组织羡慕的对象，我们可以直接为您的团队提供挽救陷入困境的项目所需的资源。这完全是他们为自己制造的选择偏差问题：向不具备内部专业知识来处理该项目的公司宣传挽救失败项目的咨询服务，然后撰写博客文章，表明您看到的 100% 项目都失败了。

sentences.en: Also refuse to help them, to guarantee they can't be converted to successful projects to keep the success number at 0%.
sentences.zh: 也拒绝帮助他们，保证他们不能转化为成功的项目，使成功率保持在0%。

blocks.html_en:
> One of their front-page selling points is that they use "ancient techniques" from books written prior to the year 2000, because presumably everything newer than that is bad?

I get what you are saying here.

At the same time, was reading Deming's last book [0] and there is a great line that I have never seen in any recent business books:

"The organizational chart is usually a pyramid. The main benefit of this style of chart is to clarify who reports to whom. Notably absent from this chart: the customer."

I've also worked at firms filled with very smart people who had no concept (or were not incentivized) to think about the Theory of Constraints, Total Quality Management or that the firm is supposed to work as a team and not a collection of fiefdoms.

What I'm saying is: just because a book was written before 2000 doesn't make it irrelevant.

0 - https://amzn.to/4waEjA2

sentences.zh: > 他们的头版卖点之一是他们使用了 2000 年之前写的书中的“古老技术”，因为大概所有比这更新的东西都是不好的？我明白你在这里所说的。同时，我在读戴明的最后一本书[0]，有一句我在最近的商业书籍中从未见过的伟大台词：“组织结构图通常是金字塔。

blocks.html_en:
> One of their front-page selling points is that they use "ancient techniques" from books written prior to the year 2000, because presumably everything newer than that is bad?

I get what you are saying here.

At the same time, was reading Deming's last book [0] and there is a great line that I have never seen in any recent business books:

"The organizational chart is usually a pyramid. The main benefit of this style of chart is to clarify who reports to whom. Notably absent from this chart: the customer."

I've also worked at firms filled with very smart people who had no concept (or were not incentivized) to think about the Theory of Constraints, Total Quality Management or that the firm is supposed to work as a team and not a collection of fiefdoms.

What I'm saying is: just because a book was written before 2000 doesn't make it irrelevant.

blocks.html_en: To be completely fair, most software projects fail, AI or not, and we've known that since before the 2000s. I'm not saying everything post-2000s is bad, but just that the core is unchanged: most software projects fail, you should expect failure, and there is no magic silver-bullet process to either anticipate or prevent failure. Agile (big A) is certainly not that process.

sentences.en: To be completely fair, most software projects fail, AI or not, and we've known that since before the 2000s.
sentences.zh: 公平地说，大多数软件项目都会失败，无论是否是人工智能，我们从 2000 年代之前就知道这一点。

sentences.en: I'm not saying everything post-2000s is bad, but just that the core is unchanged: most software projects fail, you should expect failure, and there is no magic silver-bullet process to either anticipate or prevent failure.

sentences.en: Agile (big A) is certainly not that process.
sentences.zh: 敏捷（大A）当然不是那个过程。

blocks.html_en: it's not surprising as LLM's will generally fail you when you yourself don't know what you want. it's surprising how many people and organizations just blunder around scribbling code without clear goals in mind - for such people LLM's are a net negative as they will just end up with even more scribbles and no hope to make sense of it all.

sentences.en: it's not surprising as LLM's will generally fail you when you yourself don't know what you want.
sentences.zh: 这并不奇怪，因为当你自己不知道自己想要什么时，法学硕士通常会让你失败。

sentences.en: it's surprising how many people and organizations just blunder around scribbling code without clear goals in mind - for such people LLM's are a net negative as they will just end up with even more scribbles and no hope to make sense of it all.
sentences.zh: 令人惊讶的是，有多少人和组织只是在没有明确目标的情况下胡乱写代码——对于这些人来说，LLM 完全是负面的，因为他们最终只会得到更多的潦草的代码，而且没有希望理解这一切。

blocks.html_en:
> Supposedly 70% of software projects failed even before AI.

Given the way LLMs are marketed, we should expect a vastly more favorable project success rate, not simply a close parity, and definitely not a decreased rate.

Even if we consider that the number of attempts will rise if development becomes faster, we're told to expect a greater number of projects should also succeed because they're easier. They're supposed to justify the higher costs.

It's reasonable to ask if that's the case.

sentences.en: > Supposedly 70% of software projects failed even before AI.Given the way LLMs are marketed, we should expect a vastly more favorable project success rate, not simply a close parity, and definitely not a decreased rate.Even if we consider that the number of attempts will rise if development becomes faster, we're told to expect a greater number of projects should also succeed because they're easier.
sentences.zh: > 据称，即使在人工智能出现之前，就有 70% 的软件项目失败了。考虑到 LLM 的营销方式，我们应该期待一个更加有利的项目成功率，而不仅仅是接近平价，而且绝对不会降低成功率。即使我们认为如果开发变得更快，尝试的次数会增加，我们也被告知预期更多的项目也应该成功，因为它们更容易。

sentences.en: They're supposed to justify the higher costs.It's reasonable to ask if that's the case.
sentences.zh: 他们应该证明更高的成本是合理的。询问情况是否如此是合理的。

blocks.html_en:
> Given the way LLMs are marketed, we should expect a vastly more favorable project success rate, not simply a close parity, and definitely not a decreased rate. > Even if we consider that the number of attempts will rise if development becomes faster, we're told to expect a greater number of projects should also succeed because they're easier.

I'm not sure I follow why you would think this. Prior to AI was the reason most projects failed because generating the code was too slow or too labor intensive? That would be surprising to me if it was true.

blocks.html_en:
Generating code is not, and never was, a bottleneck to projects. Understanding the problem, designing a solution, organizing resources, testing, and customer buy-in and moving targets have always represented the real issues.

Now, with LLMs, we're supposed to be able to paper over some or all of these problems.

They're supposed to be able to understand and intuitively fill the assumptions present in vague requirements, then produce working applications. They're supposed to shrink the timelines from years and months to weeks and days. They're supposed to replace human expertise with a reliable service.

Why else would I hire an LLM service over a team of humans?

sentences.en: Generating code is not, and never was, a bottleneck to projects.
sentences.zh: 生成代码现在不是、也从来不是项目的瓶颈。

sentences.en: Understanding the problem, designing a solution, organizing resources, testing, and customer buy-in and moving targets have always represented the real issues.Now, with LLMs, we're supposed to be able to paper over some or all of these problems.They're supposed to be able to understand and intuitively fill the assumptions present in vague requirements, then produce working applications.
sentences.zh: 理解问题、设计解决方案、组织资源、测试、客户认可和移动目标始终代表着真正的问题。现在，有了法学硕士，我们应该能够掩盖部分或全部这些问题。他们应该能够理解并直观地填充模糊需求中存在的假设，然后生成可行的应用程序。

sentences.en: They're supposed to shrink the timelines from years and months to weeks and days.
sentences.zh: 他们应该将时间表从几年和几个月缩短到几周和几天。

sentences.en: They're supposed to replace human expertise with a reliable service.Why else would I hire an LLM service over a team of humans?
sentences.zh: 他们应该用可靠的服务取代人类的专业知识。否则我为什么要雇佣法学硕士服务而不是人类团队呢？

sentences.zh: 他们应该将时间表从几年和几个月缩短到几周和几天。

sentences.en: They're supposed to replace human expertise with a reliable service.Why else would I hire an LLM service over a team of humans?
sentences.zh: 他们应该用可靠的服务取代人类的专业知识。否则我为什么要雇佣法学硕士服务而不是人类团队呢？

blocks.html_en: Generating code is not, and never was, a bottleneck to projects. Understanding the problem, designing a solution, organizing resources, testing, and customer buy-in and moving targets have always represented the real issues.

sentences.en: Generating code is not, and never was, a bottleneck to projects.
sentences.zh: 生成代码现在不是、也从来不是项目的瓶颈。

sentences.en: Understanding the problem, designing a solution, organizing resources, testing, and customer buy-in and moving targets have always represented the real issues.
sentences.zh: 了解问题、设计解决方案、组织资源、测试、客户认可和移动目标始终代表着真正的问题。

blocks.html_en: Now, with LLMs, we're supposed to be able to paper over some or all of these problems.

blocks.html_en: They're supposed to be able to understand and intuitively fill the assumptions present in vague requirements, then produce working applications. They're supposed to shrink the timelines from years and months to weeks and days. They're supposed to replace human expertise with a reliable service.

sentences.en: They're supposed to shrink the timelines from years and months to weeks and days.

blocks.html_en:
> They're supposed to be able to understand and intuitively fill the assumptions present in vague requirements, then produce working applications. They're supposed to shrink the timelines from years and months to weeks and days. They're supposed to replace human expertise with a reliable service.

But none of those sound like things that would change how likely any given project is to succeed. Shrinking timelines from years and months to weeks and days just means that you can fail faster and try again sooner (hopefully with lest costs), but nothing about that implies your rate of success is going to get better.

blocks.html_en:
There’s some selection bias involved especially based on their marking, but you’re misreading what they are saying.

> even within projects that we have observed in passing while doing totally unrelated work.

The kind of companies with failing projects seem to be very bad at using AI. That’s different from the normal mix of success and failures at most large companies.

sentences.zh: 这与大多数大公司的成功和失败的正常组合不同。

blocks.html_en:
Companies with projects that are doing well don't try to hire consultants who specialized in saving failing projects.

No company with a good AI strategy is going to go to the Hermit Tech website where text written in Ye Olde Font explains that they'll use ancient techniques from the 80s and 90s to make your software work and thinks, "These are the right people for our AI job!"

These people are trying to carve out a niche for themselves as being anti-modern, anti-AI, and being contrarian consultants that you can bring in when you want some external consultants to agree with you in a very specific way.

sentences.en: Companies with projects that are doing well don't try to hire consultants who specialized in saving failing projects.No company with a good AI strategy is going to go to the Hermit Tech website where text written in Ye Olde Font explains that they'll use ancient techniques from the 80s and 90s to make your software work and thinks, "These are the right people for our AI job!"These people are trying to carve out a niche for themselves as being anti-modern, anti-AI, and being contrarian consultants that you can bring in when you want some external consultants to agree with you in a very specific way.
sentences.zh: 项目进展顺利的公司不会试图聘请专门拯救失败项目的顾问。拥有良好 AI 战略的公司不会访问 Hermit Tech 网站，该网站上用 Ye Olde 字体编写的文本解释说，他们将使用 80 年代和 90 年代的古老技术来让你的软件运行，并认为，“这些人是适合我们 AI 工作的人！”这些人试图为自己开辟一个利基市场，成为反现代、反 AI 和逆向顾问当您希望一些外部顾问以非常具体的方式同意您的观点时，您可以引入这些内容。

blocks.html_en:
I think you are right on target. I’ve seen this pattern:

Company does x. X sucks, and is largely derided as being a bad idea, but it’s important-employee-bobs baby. No one wants to be the guy that tells bob that x is killing the company in some small or large way, because bob can get them fired or is protected by someone who can, so you hire consultants xyz who specialize in “transforming productivity through x management” and they come in and “transform” x into something less lovecraftian, or just explain why it’s bad in a way that makes bob look like a genius.

sentences.en: I think you are right on target.
sentences.zh: 我认为你的目标是正确的。

sentences.en: I’ve seen this pattern:Company does x.
sentences.zh: 我见过这样的模式：公司做 x。

sentences.en: X sucks, and is largely derided as being a bad idea, but it’s important-employee-bobs baby.
sentences.zh: X 很糟糕，而且在很大程度上被嘲笑为一个坏主意，但它很重要——员工鲍勃宝贝。

sentences.en: No one wants to be the guy that tells bob that x is killing the company in some small or large way, because bob can get them fired or is protected by someone who can, so you hire consultants xyz who specialize in “transforming productivity through x management” and they come in and “transform” x into something less lovecraftian, or just explain why it’s bad in a way that makes bob look like a genius.
sentences.zh: 没有人愿意成为那个告诉鲍勃 x 正在以某种或大或小的方式毁掉公司的人，因为鲍勃可以让他们被解雇，或者受到可以解雇他们的人的保护，所以你聘请了专门研究“通过 x 管理改变生产力”的顾问 xyz，他们进来将 x“转变”成不那么洛夫克拉夫特式的东西，或者只是以一种让鲍勃看起来像天才的方式解释为什么它很糟糕。

blocks.html_en:
> Companies with projects that are doing well don't try to hire consultants who specialized in saving failing projects.

Large companies where every project is going well are rare.

I agree companies with good AI strategies are unlikely to use these people, but excluding failures is just as much a bias as excluding winners. AI lets you shoot your sold in the foot even faster shouldn’t be surprising, but it’s still something to consider.

sentences.zh: 人工智能可以让你更快地搬起石头砸自己的脚，这并不奇怪，但这仍然是需要考虑的事情。

blocks.html_en:
I think "your everyday liberal professional" is more likely to be anti-AI because it is being advertised as a direct threat to their livelihoods, rather than for any more abstract reason. The latter includes both political allegiance stuff and the reasons those professionals would likely cite, like its energy requirements or the economic damage if the bubble bursts.

Whether or not you think it likely that this threat will materialize is beside the point. Workers never like it when someone is advertising that they will put you out of work.

sentences.zh: 工人们从来不喜欢有人打广告说他们会让你失业。

blocks.html_en:
That observation[0] isn't particularly useful for arguing that it's a political allegiance thing. A lower fraction of voters in the pro-tech part of California (SFO, roughly) voted for Trump than, say, in NYC. Your observation cuts against the argument that political allegiance is the main driver, not in favor of it.

[0]- Assuming it's true, which in my experience it isn't especially, but this isn't the best argument.

blocks.html_en:
You have no idea, you must not know that they are afraid of Waymos out in the northeast, they think they are evil or something, I'm not even joking.

Anyway, I didn't say it was a political thing I said it was a cultural thing. The average liberal is not someone who thinks primarily in political terms, they are just going about their lives unreflectively and they genuinely believe in the moral weight of their feelings, which are their primary guide. Now, I'm not saying that human life is not generally composed of its daily concerns, which tend to rule us, but for the average liberal politics is no different from acting on basic urges; like a mouse chasing the next piece of cheese in a maze, they cannot see that no turn will ever lead to their escape; that so long as they continue to chase the very next thing, they are trapped in constraints that have been laid out for them in advance. Like the conservatives of the 80s, there is always one more moral panic, one more thing that you're supposed to be upset about, one more cause to fight for. There is no stopping to think why this is something that is supposed to upset me, why is this something that needs to change. They are not able to go just one step beyond common opinion and wonder why there are so few avenues for them in the first place, they are not able to question why the maximum content of their decisions is good or evil, just or unjust, left or right. Yes any decision is binary, but one must be able to choose that for themselves, be able to consider the decision to leave the maze, even if they are, in the end, no more powerful than all the other rats, and there might not be any cheese outside of it; and there might be no end to the maze, and even very far from what you thought was the exit lay the bodies of those who attempted to leave before.

But anyway this is what I mean by primarily cultural. Liberals "drink matcha," go to running clubs, obsess over "consent" (they now have a term "weaponized consent," how absurd! that they are just realizing now that consent is always given within certain relations of power...) Whatever politics they have is on the same plane, they are doing it because its something to be done. Someone who thinks primarily in terms of politics, that is, in terms of the social dynamics of power, would never call themselves a liberal.

sentences.en: You have no idea, you must not know that they are afraid of Waymos out in the northeast, they think they are evil or something, I'm not even joking.Anyway, I didn't say it was a political thing I said it was a cultural thing.
sentences.zh: 你不知道，你一定不知道他们害怕东北的Waymos，他们认为他们是邪恶的什么的，我不是在开玩笑。不管怎样，我没有说这是一个政治问题，我说这是一个文化问题。

sentences.en: The average liberal is not someone who thinks primarily in political terms, they are just going about their lives unreflectively and they genuinely believe in the moral weight of their feelings, which are their primary guide.
sentences.zh: 一般的自由主义者并不是主要从政治角度思考的人，他们只是不加反思地过着自己的生活，他们真诚地相信自己感情的道德分量，这是他们的主要指导。

sentences.en: Now, I'm not saying that human life is not generally composed of its daily concerns, which tend to rule us, but for the average liberal politics is no different from acting on basic urges; like a mouse chasing the next piece of cheese in a maze, they cannot see that no turn will ever lead to their escape; that so long as they continue to chase the very next thing, they are trapped in constraints that have been laid out for them in advance.
sentences.zh: 现在，我并不是说人类生活通常不是由日常事务组成的，这些事务往往会统治我们，但对于一般的自由主义政治来说，这与根据基本冲动行事没有什么不同；就像老鼠在迷宫中追逐下一块奶酪一样，他们看不到任何转弯都无法逃脱；只要他们继续追逐下一件事情，他们就会陷入预先为他们安排的约束之中。

sentences.en: Like the conservatives of the 80s, there is always one more moral panic, one more thing that you're supposed to be upset about, one more cause to fight for.
sentences.zh: 就像 80 年代的保守派一样，总会有更多的道德恐慌，又一件你应该感到不安的事情，又一个值得为之奋斗的理由。

sentences.en: There is no stopping to think why this is something that is supposed to upset me, why is this something that needs to change.
sentences.zh: 我不停地思考为什么这应该让我心烦意乱，为什么这需要改变。

sentences.en: They are not able to go just one step beyond common opinion and wonder why there are so few avenues for them in the first place, they are not able to question why the maximum content of their decisions is good or evil, just or unjust, left or right.
sentences.zh: 他们无法超越普遍意见一步，并想知道为什么他们的途径如此之少，他们无法质疑为什么他们的决定的最大内容是善或恶，正义或不正义，左或右。

sentences.en: Yes any decision is binary, but one must be able to choose that for themselves, be able to consider the decision to leave the maze, even if they are, in the end, no more powerful than all the other rats, and there might not be any cheese outside of it; and there might be no end to the maze, and even very far from what you thought was the exit lay the bodies of those who attempted to leave before.But anyway this is what I mean by primarily cultural.
sentences.zh: 是的，任何决定都是二元的，但一个人必须能够为自己做出选择，能够考虑离开迷宫的决定，即使他们最终并不比所有其他老鼠更强大，而且外面可能没有任何奶酪；迷宫可能没有尽头，甚至在离你想象的出口很远的地方，躺着那些之前试图离开的人的尸体。但无论如何，这就是我所说的主要文化的意思。

sentences.en: Liberals "drink matcha," go to running clubs, obsess over "consent" (they now have a term "weaponized consent," how absurd!
sentences.zh: 自由主义者“喝抹茶”，去跑步俱乐部，痴迷于“同意”（他们现在有一个术语“武器化的同意”，多么荒谬！

sentences.en: that they are just realizing now that consent is always given within certain relations of power...)
sentences.zh: 他们现在才意识到，在某些权力关系中总是会给予同意……）

sentences.en: Whatever politics they have is on the same plane, they are doing it because its something to be done.
sentences.zh: 无论他们有什么政治观点，他们都在同一平面上，他们这样做是因为这是要做的事情。

sentences.en: Someone who thinks primarily in terms of politics, that is, in terms of the social dynamics of power, would never call themselves a liberal.
sentences.zh: 主要从政治角度思考的人，即从权力的社会动力角度思考的人，永远不会称自己为自由主义者。

sentences.zh: 就像 80 年代的保守派一样，总会有更多的道德恐慌，又一件你应该感到不安的事情，又一个值得为之奋斗的理由。

sentences.en: There is no stopping to think why this is something that is supposed to upset me, why is this something that needs to change.
sentences.zh: 我不停地思考为什么这应该让我心烦意乱，为什么这需要改变。

sentences.en: They are not able to go just one step beyond common opinion and wonder why there are so few avenues for them in the first place, they are not able to question why the maximum content of their decisions is good or evil, just or unjust, left or right.
sentences.zh: 他们无法超越普遍意见一步，并想知道为什么他们的途径如此之少，他们无法质疑为什么他们的决定的最大内容是善或恶，正义或不正义，左或右。

sentences.en: Yes any decision is binary, but one must be able to choose that for themselves, be able to consider the decision to leave the maze, even if they are, in the end, no more powerful than all the other rats, and there might not be any cheese outside of it; and there might be no end to the maze, and even very far from what you thought was the exit lay the bodies of those who attempted to leave before.But anyway this is what I mean by primarily cultural.
sentences.zh: 是的，任何决定都是二元的，但一个人必须能够为自己做出选择，能够考虑离开迷宫的决定，即使他们最终并不比所有其他老鼠更强大，而且外面可能没有任何奶酪；迷宫可能没有尽头，甚至在离你想象的出口很远的地方，躺着那些之前试图离开的人的尸体。但无论如何，这就是我所说的主要文化的意思。

sentences.en: Liberals "drink matcha," go to running clubs, obsess over "consent" (they now have a term "weaponized consent," how absurd!
sentences.zh: 自由主义者“喝抹茶”，去跑步俱乐部，痴迷于“同意”（他们现在有一个术语“武器化的同意”，多么荒谬！

sentences.en: that they are just realizing now that consent is always given within certain relations of power...)
sentences.zh: 他们现在才意识到，在某些权力关系中总是会给予同意……）

sentences.en: Whatever politics they have is on the same plane, they are doing it because its something to be done.
sentences.zh: 无论他们有什么政治观点，他们都在同一平面上，他们这样做是因为这是要做的事情。

sentences.en: Someone who thinks primarily in terms of politics, that is, in terms of the social dynamics of power, would never call themselves a liberal.
sentences.zh: 主要从政治角度思考的人，即从权力的社会动力角度思考的人，永远不会称自己为自由主义者。

blocks.html_en: You have no idea, you must not know that they are afraid of Waymos out in the northeast, they think they are evil or something, I'm not even joking.

sentences.en: You have no idea, you must not know that they are afraid of Waymos out in the northeast, they think they are evil or something, I'm not even joking.
sentences.zh: 你不知道，你一定不知道他们害怕东北的Waymos，他们认为他们是邪恶的什么的，我不是在开玩笑。

blocks.html_en: Anyway, I didn't say it was a political thing I said it was a cultural thing. The average liberal is not someone who thinks primarily in political terms, they are just going about their lives unreflectively and they genuinely believe in the moral weight of their feelings, which are their primary guide. Now, I'm not saying that human life is not generally composed of its daily concerns, which tend to rule us, but for the average liberal politics is no different from acting on basic urges; like a mouse chasing the next piece of cheese in a maze, they cannot see that no turn will ever lead to their escape; that so long as they continue to chase the very next thing, they are trapped in constraints that have been laid out for them in advance. Like the conservatives of the 80s, there is always one more moral panic, one more thing that you're supposed to be upset about, one more cause to fight for. There is no stopping to think why this is something that is supposed to upset me, why is this something that needs to change. They are not able to go just one step beyond common opinion and wonder why there are so few avenues for them in the first place, they are not able to question why the maximum content of their decisions is good or evil, just or unjust, left or right. Yes any decision is binary, but one must be able to choose that for themselves, be able to consider the decision to leave the maze, even if they are, in the end, no more powerful than all the other rats, and there might not be any cheese outside of it; and there might be no end to the maze, and even very far from what you thought was the exit lay the bodies of those who attempted to leave before.

sentences.en: Anyway, I didn't say it was a political thing I said it was a cultural thing.
sentences.zh: 不管怎样，我没有说这是一个政治问题，我说这是一个文化问题。

blocks.html_en:
You did claim that it was political:

> Its just the thing that upsets me the most about your everyday liberal professional, how much they hate AI just because Trump is pro AI

That's what I'm refuting here -- that people here dislike AI just to be contrary to Trump. This is implausible.

And trying to lecture a life long New Englander who currently lives within the MBTA zone on what the culture is like in the northeast is maybe not the best look. We aren't afraid of Waymos here; the only people I've met who weren't jealous of them were taxi or Uber drivers. That supports my hypothesis, not yours.

sentences.zh: 你确实声称这是政治性的：>这只是你日常自由派专业人士最让我不安的事情，他们多么讨厌人工智能，只是因为特朗普支持人工智能——这就是我在这里反驳的——这里的人们不喜欢人工智能只是因为与特朗普相反。

blocks.html_en:
I'm not sure why my experience differs from yours. When I was in the northeast recently I got into heated arguments with people, one person said they "hated Waymo" and refused to elaborate (but also said they used AI everyday for work), another said flat out that technology is evil, so I asked them if poor people should go without electricity. Then they restricted their argument to AI is evil, and that was a more nuanced (if not trite) debate, which they eventually conceded. But not without some gnawing and thrashing!

But I don't think that hating AI or technology or whatever to be contrary to Trump is a genuine political statement, its just reaction. It involves politics in the same way that everything we do involves politics, but it is not "consciously" political in the same way that a baby demanding care from an overworked mother is also not consciously political. Baby wants milk, your average liberal wants to feel self-righteous (they would honestly do better with the milk). Someone can claim anything they want is political, it does not mean they are doing it for genuinely political reasons, they are probably just doing it because they fear the censure of their friends and the dissolution of their social life. Not engaging with something because you are afraid of how it could harm you personally, consciously or no, is not an objective way of making a decision that effects society more broadly. Like, a baby will just die if it doesn't get milk, you won't necessarily die because some of your friends stop talking to you.

sentences.en: I'm not sure why my experience differs from yours.
sentences.zh: 我不确定为什么我的经历与你的不同。
